{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 2-year-old girl with recurrent urinary tract infections, bilateral renal pelvis dilation on ultrasound, and retrograde flow of contrast into the ureters during micturition on voiding cystourethrography. These findings are consistent with vesicoureteral reflux (VUR). VUR is a known cause of reflux nephropathy (RN), which leads to chronic interstitial nephritis, tubular atrophy, and renal scarring. The histologic findings in reflux nephropathy are well-documented in the provided documents. Document [0] states that in reflux nephropathy, the renal biopsy may show global glomerular hypertrophy, periglomerular fibrosis, chronic interstitial nephritis, patchy interstitial scarring, extensive tubular atrophy, and secondary FSGS. Document [8] further elaborates that VUR results in chronic interstitial nephritis, tubular atrophy, and renal scarring. Among the options, option C, 'Cortical thinning with tubular atrophy,' directly aligns with these histologic features. Cortical thinning is a consequence of chronic interstitial nephritis and scarring, and tubular atrophy is a hallmark of long-standing reflux nephropathy. Option A (glomerular crescents) is typical of crescentic glomerulonephritis, not reflux nephropathy. Option B (matrix expansion with mesangial proliferation) is characteristic of mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis, such as IgA nephropathy. Option D (thickened glomerular capillary loops) is not a typical feature of reflux nephropathy. Therefore, the most likely histologic finding in this patient is cortical thinning with tubular atrophy.", "answer_choice": "C"}
